evals.prompt: We're spending about $1,200/month on OpenAI for a support ticket classifier. It uses GPT-4o for everything — classifying tickets, generating responses, and summarizing conversations. We get about 10,000 tickets a day. How can I cut costs without losing too much accuracy?
evals.expected_output: A concrete plan that identifies per-module model assignment as the key lever (classification is simple enough for a cheap model, generation may need the expensive one). Should include code showing dspy.context or per-module LM assignment, a cost audit step, and a recommendation to measure quality before and after.

assertions.name: assigns_per_module_lms
assertions.description: Shows how to assign different LMs to different pipeline steps using dspy.context or module.lm

assertions.name: uses_cheap_model_for_classification
assertions.description: Recommends a cheaper model (gpt-4o-mini, Haiku, or open-source) for the classification step

assertions.name: includes_cost_audit
assertions.description: Shows how to check current token usage with dspy.inspect_history or similar

assertions.name: measures_quality
assertions.description: Includes a dspy.evaluate or metric check to verify quality holds after switching models

assertions.name: provider_agnostic
assertions.description: LM examples include alternative provider comments, not just OpenAI

evals.prompt: I built a RAG system for internal docs search. It retrieves 10 passages and feeds them to GPT-4o to answer questions. The prompts are huge because of all the context. My boss wants me to cut the API bill in half. I have no labeled data.
evals.expected_output: Focus on reducing prompt length: fewer retrieved passages (k=3-5 instead of 10), concise signatures, and switching to a cheaper model. Should mention cascading (try cheap model first) as an advanced option. Should NOT suggest fine-tuning since there's no labeled data.

assertions.name: reduces_retrieval_k
assertions.description: Recommends reducing the number of retrieved passages

assertions.name: suggests_cheaper_model
assertions.description: Recommends trying a cheaper model for the generation step

assertions.name: addresses_prompt_length
assertions.description: Mentions simplifying signatures or reducing few-shot demos to cut token usage

assertions.name: no_finetune_without_data
assertions.description: Does NOT recommend fine-tuning since the user has no labeled data

assertions.name: suggests_cascading_or_routing
assertions.description: Mentions smart routing or cascading as an option for further savings

evals.prompt: I have a content moderation pipeline that checks user posts for 6 policy violations. Right now every post goes through GPT-4o with ChainOfThought. 80% of posts are totally fine and don't violate anything. We process 50,000 posts/day and it's costing us a fortune. I have 2,000 labeled examples.
evals.expected_output: Should identify the 80/20 pattern as ideal for smart routing — use a cheap model to screen obvious non-violations, only escalate flagged posts to the expensive model. Should suggest using Predict instead of ChainOfThought for the initial screen. May mention fine-tuning as an option given the 2,000 examples.

assertions.name: uses_routing_pattern
assertions.description: Implements a complexity/difficulty router that sends easy cases to a cheap model

assertions.name: leverages_80_20_distribution
assertions.description: Explicitly addresses that 80% of traffic is simple and can be handled cheaply

assertions.name: uses_predict_for_screening
assertions.description: Recommends Predict instead of ChainOfThought for the initial cheap screening step

assertions.name: mentions_finetune_option
assertions.description: Mentions fine-tuning as a viable option given 2,000 labeled examples

assertions.name: includes_dspy_code
assertions.description: Provides working DSPy code for the routing pattern, not just prose advice
